topic_hint: Speaker's personal struggle to understand software messaging and realizing its vagueness.

examples.example_text: The speaker's personal experience of entering software with no prior background and initially feeling inadequate when reading company homepages, only to later realize the messaging itself was vague.
examples.concept_illustrated: The initial struggle with vague product messaging leading to the realization of a positioning problem.

examples.example_text: Clary's homepage using vague phrases like 'Revenue orchestration platform,' 'Drive predictable growth powered by AI,' and 'Run revenue.'
examples.concept_illustrated: Examples of confusing and meaningless marketing copy that results from poor positioning.

examples.example_text: A hypothetical (but representative) example of vague messaging: 'A better way to revenue, the AI revenue platform,' mentioning RevOps, CS, and sellers.
examples.concept_illustrated: Further illustration of generalized, buzzword-heavy product descriptions.

examples.example_text: Slack's tagline 'Where work happens,' which the speaker sarcastically misinterprets as a co-working space.
examples.concept_illustrated: Illustrates how even popular products can have vague, undescriptive taglines due to poor positioning.

examples.example_text: The movie 'Inception' used as an analogy for the goal of positioning – getting an idea (your product) firmly planted in the customer's mind.
examples.concept_illustrated: The concept of 'mental availability' as the objective of positioning.

examples.example_text: Celsius energy drink's journey from a struggling weight loss drink to a dominating energy drink, achieving success through repositioning without changing its core ingredients.
examples.concept_illustrated: The power and impact of effective repositioning; how changing target audience and market perception can transform a product's success.

examples.example_text: YouTube initially starting as an online dating site and successfully repositioning as a general video sharing platform.
examples.concept_illustrated: Another example of successful repositioning leading to massive growth.

key_ideas.idea: Many software companies use vague, unhelpful, and meaningless language on their homepages.
The root cause of confusing product messaging is a 'positioning problem,' not simply a 'copy problem'.

Positioning challenges differ based on company size: large companies struggle due to internal politics, executive egos, and expensive branding agencies, while small startups struggle due to the inherent difficulty of explaining new products.
Positioning involves answering three core questions: 'Who is your product for?', 'What is it?', and 'Why is it better?'.
The goal of positioning is to achieve 'mental availability,' so customers recall your product in their moment of need or struggle.
A product can be massively successful by repositioning for a different market, even without changing its core features or ingredients (e.g., Celsius, YouTube).
Homepages should be concise; lengthy explanations (e.g., 30-45 minutes) will cause potential customers to lose interest.

mentioned_concepts.context: Mentioned as a term the speaker initially didn't understand when entering software.

mentioned_concepts.context: Mentioned as a phrase the speaker initially hadn't heard when entering software.

mentioned_concepts.context: Mentioned as a concept related to vague product descriptions.

mentioned_concepts.context: Mentioned as a role related to vague product descriptions.

supporting_concepts.name: Repositioning
supporting_concepts.role: Demonstrates the power and impact of effective positioning by showing how changing a product's market perception (without changing the product) can lead to success.

supporting_concepts.name: Mental Availability
supporting_concepts.role: Explains the ultimate goal of good positioning: to ensure customers recall the product in their moment of need.

supporting_concepts.name: Copywriting
supporting_concepts.role: Contrasted with positioning to clarify that vague messaging is a strategic positioning problem, not merely a problem of writing style.

supporting_concepts.name: Competitive Differentiation
supporting_concepts.role: Implied as a key outcome and goal of answering the 'why is it better' question in positioning.

supporting_concepts.name: Company Size
supporting_concepts.role: Used to explain the different origins and challenges of positioning problems faced by large corporations versus small startups.

transcript: When I came into software, I didn't have software as my background. Had no idea what a B2B company meant. Never heard the phrase SAS. I came into it late late in life. And what would happen is when I would read people's homepages of these software companies, I would say, I can't wait until I get smart enough to understand what these people are talking about. And then after a little while, I realized, wait a minute, I think they're actually just not saying anything at all. And just some of my favorite egregious examples. Uh, this is a great one. Revenue orchestration platform because who doesn't want to orchestrate some revenue, right? Drive predictable growth powered by AI. See why these teams rely on Clarity to run revenue. Run revenue. It sounds like an interesting type of software to just print money, I guess, for your business. I'm hoping all of you guys are using Clary to print your money like me. Uh, another good one. This is a great one. A better way to revenue, the AI revenue platform. And as you can see, there's some little folks around the side. They're doing something something about RevOps. There's a CS, a seller. So, just really cool stuff. And then even like the products that we all use today, uh they also have like amazingly helpful descriptive copy of what they do, right? Where work happens. It's a cool co-working space that just started in my town. It's called Slack. You guys could check it out if you're ever in Chicago. Uh that's where all my work happens. I go there. It's on the third floor of uh uh one of the buildings downtown. So in reality, it's not a copy problem that leads to this type of messaging. It's a positioning problem and the problem originates in different places depending on the size of your company. All the examples that I shared are very large companies and they end up with that kind of gobbledygook because they have a bunch of executives with really big titles, giant comp packages and multi-million dollar branding agencies that are trying to puff them up and they end up with after these long engagements with stuff that doesn't really say anything. and none of them are super happy with it, but they pat themselves on the back and they're like, "This is great. I get to leave my mark in this company and then I'll go somewhere else and destroy the next company's branding." So, it's a kind of fun cycle for the big companies. But in the early days, if you're a smaller startup founder, like many of you are in this room, you likely also struggle with positioning, but for less annoying reasons. It's you're building a new product. You're trying to get it out into the world, and it's not always easy to explain it to people. And I've even chat with some people here. It takes a little bit to walk through all the different aspects of your product to get people to understand exactly what it does and why people would need it. And the problem is when you take that, you know, 1 to two to five to 30 45 minute explanation to explain the product. If you tried to put that somewhere like a homepage, all of a sudden you've lost people because nobody reads homepages for 45 minutes except me and my co-founder Rob, which is uh very sad. But in general, what is positioning for us? Positioning, it's answering three questions. It's the act of deciding who is your product for, what is it, and why is it better? And the whole goal of positioning is kind of like that movie Inception. You want to get a spot in the minds of your customers where they think of you like the mental availability that when they're in a moment of struggle, they would say, "Oh, I remember there's a product that solves this specific problem or I'm stuck in this broken process. I think there's probably software that I could get that would make it better." And the idea with positioning is actively choosing who your product is for, what it is, and why it's better to try to get a spot in their minds so that they think of you and they don't think of your competitors. But to give you some fun examples of repositioning that maybe you familiar with, maybe not. How many of you have ever drank Celsius on the right, the energy drink? Some of you. Come on, you guys are working late nights. You're drinking energy drinks. Don't f You're not fooling any of me. Uh, I have a young new nine-week old baby and we are up multiple times in the night. And so in the morning, I will usually have Thank you. I'll usually have a uh Celsius and I used to go downstairs to get the Celsius from the fridge and then I would wake up our three-year-old toddler on my way down to get the energy drink. And so I actually bought a very small mini fridge to put right next to the bed so that I can pop open an energy drink first thing in the morning and then get the day started without waiting waking up my toddler. But a lot of people don't know that Celsius began as a weight loss drink. It was positioned for people who were trying to lose weight. They had all these things about burning calories, reducing body fat, and they were not doing so great. And then they repositioned for the energy drink market, which is a traditionally very masculine dominated place. If you think of Monster, energy drink, ghost, uh, all these kind of really intense things, Red Bulls doing all these crazy physical activities, they repositioned as a kind of more feminine and lighter drink and they're absolutely dominating. So, the power of repositioning, it's the same product, the exact same ingredients, just repositioned for a new group. And it's a difference between being kind of a floundering little drink company, being a giant powerhouse. Um, another one, you know, from from software examples, some of you probably know, YouTube started as an online dating site where you would post videos of yourself and people would see it. They repositioned the software for just a general video sharing. Huge, huge

topic_hint: Examples of confusing and vague software descriptions on company homepages, including 'Revenue orchestration platform' and 'Where work happens'.

examples.example_text: YouTube started as an online dating site and repositioned to general video sharing, leading to huge success.
Notion started as an app builder, failed, and repositioned as a productivity tool, achieving success by 'Trojan horsing' app building.
Twitch narrowed its positioning from general streaming to a gaming streaming platform, leading to significant success.
Miro calls itself an 'innovation workspace' but customers perceive it as a 'digital whiteboard with sticky notes and arrows,' illustrating the 'inside the bottle' problem.
Netflix's product suite includes original content, licensed content, and mobile phone games, showing a 'Frankenstein' product suite due to potential positioning issues as it grew.
Fletch (speaker's company) helps companies answer positioning questions (Who, What, Why Better) and applies it to homepages.

Loom is used as a primary case study to illustrate the entire positioning framework, from identifying its target workflow (providing updates), competitive alternative (meetings), problem (meetings are bad), product category (screen recorder), and differentiation (binary vs. meetings).
Calendly built a giant business ($270M/year) on a tiny workflow: scheduling meetings, demonstrating the power of low-level workflow segmentation and product-led growth.
Gainsight covers the end-to-end customer success workflow, illustrating a high-level workflow strategy with longer sales cycles.
Clary (mentioned as 'Claries of the world') as a hypothetical example of a company that might incorrectly claim 'increase revenue' as a workflow.
Slack's competitor was email, not other messaging apps, demonstrating that competitive alternatives can be different product categories.
Around positioned directly against Zoom as a 'Zoom replacement' (and was acquired), illustrating a direct vendor competitive alternative.
Commour positions by saying the problem is with the workflow itself (stop outbound sales, do warm intros instead).
Lemlist positions by saying the problem is with the competitive tools (other sales engagement tools have bad email deliverability, ours is good).
E-signature software (DocuSign, PandaDoc) is used as an example of a well-known, mature product category requiring little education.
Product-Led Sales is cited as an example of an emerging category that failed to gain traction despite VC funding, illustrating the risk of choosing an emerging category.
Servikit (a company Fletch worked with) considered positioning as a 'survey tool' (mature) versus a 'customer feedback platform' (emerging), demonstrating category trade-offs.
Superhuman differentiates 'by degree' by being an email client that allows users to get through their inbox 'way faster' than Gmail.
DuckDuckGo differentiates 'binarily' against Google by promising 'never track you' due to Google's business model being built on tracking.
Hypothetical scenario for Loom's supporting arguments if it were competing in video capture, referencing tools like iMovie or Final Cut for editing.

key_ideas.idea: Many successful companies (YouTube, Notion, Twitch) achieved success by repositioning their products.
Founders often struggle with positioning because they are 'inside the bottle' and cannot see their product from an external, customer perspective.
Customer perception of a product (e.g., Miro as a digital whiteboard) often differs from the founder's aspirational vision (e.g., innovation workspace).

Signs of a positioning problem include: unclear website messaging, lack of differentiation in competitive markets, inability to choose a target group, a large education gap for new products, and a 'Frankenstein' product suite.
Effective positioning requires answering three core questions: Who is your product for? What is it? Why is it better?
The homepage is crucial for internal and external alignment of positioning; positioning isn't 'real' until it's on the homepage.
For B2B software, the most important element for Ideal Customer Profile (ICP) segmentation is the workflow the software augments, supports, or replaces, rather than traditional firmographics.
All software is workflow software.
Workflows can be high-level (complex, long sales cycle, high price) or low-level (simple, fast sales cycle, lower price, potential for product-led growth and broader appeal).
A product's competitive alternative may not be a direct product competitor but an entirely different process or tool (e.g., meetings for Loom, email for Slack).
Problems solved by a product can be inherent in the workflow itself or in the existing competitive alternative(s).

Choosing a product category involves trade-offs: mature categories offer low education but high competition, while emerging categories offer buzz but high education, pricing uncertainty, and risk of category failure.
Differentiation can be 'by degree' (doing the same thing 10x better/faster/cheaper) or 'binary' (doing things competitors simply do not).
Homepage content should be designed for quick skimming (15-30 second product demo) rather than detailed reading, using clear headlines and concise product highlights (feature, capability, benefit).
Color-coding homepage copy to match strategic positioning elements ensures messaging consistency and prevents accidental changes to positioning.

mentioned_concepts.context: Mentioned as often missing key workflow ingredients

mentioned_concepts.context: Implications of workflow granularity on GTM strategy

mentioned_concepts.context: Affected by the level of workflow chosen for segmentation

mentioned_concepts.name: Product-Led Growth (PLG)
mentioned_concepts.context: A potential strategy for products addressing low-level workflows

mentioned_concepts.name: VC Money
mentioned_concepts.context: Discussed in the context of funding emerging product categories that may fail

mentioned_concepts.name: Copywriting
mentioned_concepts.context: Distinguished as a separate step after core positioning is established

mentioned_concepts.name: Features, Capabilities, Benefits
mentioned_concepts.context: Elements used in creating product highlights on a homepage

mentioned_concepts.name: PowerPoint/Google Docs
mentioned_concepts.context: Tools where positioning often gets stored and forgotten, contrasting with the homepage

supporting_concepts.name: Repositioning
supporting_concepts.role: Illustrates the impact and necessity of changing positioning

supporting_concepts.name: Founder's Bias ('Inside the Bottle')
supporting_concepts.role: Explains a common challenge in achieving effective positioning

supporting_concepts.name: Customer Perception vs. Founder Vision
supporting_concepts.role: Highlights the disconnect that leads to positioning problems

supporting_concepts.name: Signs of a Positioning Problem
supporting_concepts.role: Provides diagnostic indicators for poor positioning

supporting_concepts.name: Positioning Framework
supporting_concepts.role: Presents a structured approach to defining a product's positioning (Who, What, Why Better)

supporting_concepts.name: Homepage as an Alignment Tool
supporting_concepts.role: Emphasizes the critical role of the homepage in operationalizing and communicating positioning

supporting_concepts.name: Workflow-Based Segmentation
supporting_concepts.role: Explains a key method for identifying the target audience for B2B software

supporting_concepts.name: Competitive Alternative
supporting_concepts.role: Identifies the actual competitors, which may not be direct product rivals

supporting_concepts.name: Problem Framing
supporting_concepts.role: Describes how to articulate the specific problem a product solves, either in the workflow or the alternative

supporting_concepts.name: Product Category Choice
supporting_concepts.role: Discusses the strategic decision of positioning within a mature or emerging product category

supporting_concepts.name: Differentiation Strategy
supporting_concepts.role: Outlines two types of product differentiation: by degree and binary

supporting_concepts.name: Homepage Messaging Structure
supporting_concepts.role: Provides best practices for organizing and writing homepage content based on positioning

So those are the kind of two different problem framings. Loom, they chose to put the problem not in the workflow. They didn't say don't update your team anymore. They said we're going to segment by people who are having meetings and think that the meetings are bad. that if we're sitting in meetings all day and people are thinking this could have been an email, they're like maybe it could have been a loom. We could just send a quick screen recording. So that's how they sort of built their core segmentation and again it doesn't have any of the traditional stuff that we normally think of in that strategy but that was a very very powerful strategy of segmentation of what types of people they want to go after. So they answered that question of who it's for based on those three elements. Then we get to the second question that you have to answer. What is your product? What is it? And this is where we start to talk about product category. And a lot of you have probably struggled with this in your own businesses, especially if you're doing something that isn't just in a very known well-known category. And you thought, what should I call myself? I kind of have elements of this tool. I kind of have elements of that tool. How do I actually explain it? Should I make up something? Should I put myself in a mature category even if it doesn't fit? This is one of those tradeoff decision moments. You can either choose the well-known mature category and try to expand what it would include to get you in there or you can put yourself in what we would call like one of the emerging sexy categories. And there are tradeoffs with both of these. If you do the mature one, it's very little education. People get it. If you say I'm making e- signature software, everyone knows what e- signature is. You can basically start the conversation there. Very little education required. The problem now you're going to be competing with all the big dogs. You're going after DocYsine, Panda Do and people are going to say, "Well, what makes you better than these giant incumbents?" And you have to have a pretty dang good answer to those types of questions. On the flip side, you could put yourself in the emerging category, which might be like getting more buzz, and and put yourself there and sort of say, "I think this category is going to grow and we want to get in on the ground floor." But there's a lot of risk of this. there's a big amount of education that you have to explain before they'll actually care what you're doing. And a lot of people, right, they won't have anything to compare you to. So there's no real price framing. So if you're worried that it's going to be too cheap or something like that, that doesn't really exist in this new category. But there's also the big risk that the category just won't pan out. That people will put all this money to say this category deserves to exist. It should live in people's budgets. We should fund it. And then it could potentially completely uh peter out. There's a example of a product category from about a year or two ago called productled sales which was if you're a productled company and you want to move up market. You could basically have software that looks at all your productled users and see where are their concentrations of different companies and who might be a good hand raiser and potentially you could get that uh those people on a sales call easier using these signals. Product led sales did not take and all the biggest productled sales companies have kind of pivoted or gone out of business and so there was tons and tons of VC money poured into it. It didn't really take. If you had positioned yourself in this sexy emerging category if it doesn't get legs now you're kind of up the river and without a whatever you know the phrases I we're in New Orleans, right? Uh so an example this is a company we worked with called Servikit. They were going after people who were collecting customer feedback. That was the workflowbased segment and they were going after people who were using survey tools to collect the customer feedback. And so their one option was to just call themselves a survey tool and say we are a brand new type of survey tool because we have this extra differentiated capability that we can collect customer feedback not just with the surveys but by listening to what customers are saying on other public channels. So there's some downsides, there's some upsides, right? They're basically saying, "We think that this is going to matter to people who are buying survey tools. We think the definition could stretch and we could basically say, yes, this is going to be a really cool thing that they want to do some risks. Will people actually care about that? Will people running surveys actually care about listening in other places?" Option two, they could position in the emerging category and say, "There's this new thing called a customer feedback platform. You don't want a survey tool. That's so old school. You need a broader customer feedback platform that gets feedback from everywhere. Yes, it does surveys. That's just a little small corner of the product. It'll also see what they're saying on the review sites and in your sales calls and collects it from all different places. Again, there's risks and trade-offs with either of these approaches. This risk here is that like nobody knows what a customer feedback platform is. No one's shopping for one. No one cares. No one will want to buy one. Uh but potentially maybe they could catch the wave on the front end and get some of the benefits of that. So, you really want to choose a product category and understand the trade-offs. We'll take Loom. They did not invent a product category. They just said, "We're a screen recorder." It was very unsexy, but they were bringing it to a group of people who didn't traditionally shop for screen recorders in a sort of an interesting use case way that ended up really paying off for them, you know, in the pandemic. The last piece is why are you different? What is your differentiation that makes someone want to pick you over the competition? And differentiation really comes in two different flavors. We could think about one, it's called differentiation by degree. It's basically we do the same thing as the other companies, but we do it way better or faster or cheaper. This is when you hear about people having a 10x difference. This what they're talking about. If you're going to make a product and you use this different this differentiation strategy, you better be 10x better for this to work. On the flip side, you could have binary differentiation, which is where you do things that the other people simply do not do. And so, I'll give you an example. Superhum uh they are an email client and they basically say you can get through your email list your inbox way faster with superhuman than with any other email client. It's a degree of difference. They have all these keyboard shortcuts and snippets and things that let you fly through your inbox way faster than if you were using Gmail. But again, they're still doing the same fundamental things as Gmail just in a faster, better way. On the flip side, if you're duck go, your differentiation is against someone like Google and basically you're saying Google, their whole business model is built on tracking you and taking your data, selling it to other people. We will never track you. It's a binary difference. If you're a privacy-minded individual, this is forcing you to choose one or the other. So, sometimes binary can be stronger, but sometimes the other one as well, differentiation by degree, can be stronger. And so if we pull this all together, we basically have the positioning statement for Loom that would give us the ammo to then write the homepage. So we would say, well, who is Loom for? Loom is for people who are providing regular updates to their co-workers. And the way they do this is by having these meetings. And the problem with meetings is that they're long, they're a waste of time, and they're super boring. We are a screen recorder. And what we let you do is send very quick update uh videos to your co-workers. Takes you a couple seconds. It records your screen and your face at the same time. And you can share them, see who's using it or who's actually watching it. And you're going to cut down the amount of time that you spend in meetings by a ton. Right? So they were doing a very a binary difference a binary differentiation very different than meetings altogether. So this gives us our core positioning which then needs to make its way onto the homepage. This is going to be a really not helpful slide based on how small everything is. So, uh you can ask me after I can send this to you a blank one and we could go through it even a little bit more in depth. But essentially what you're seeing is once you've made the core highle positioning argument, you want to make supporting arguments and you can basically just go down a level of specificity and rather than saying here's the pitch for the product, you would say here's the pitch for different specific features. So you could say this is why this specific screen recording feature is so helpful. And what you would do is you would basically walk it out against the alternative. And so for example, if Loom, let's say they hadn't positioned against meetings, let's say they were playing in the video capture space and they were like, here are all the tools you would need to do this without Loom. First, you're going to have to record your update. And so you're probably going to need some type of screen recorder, a webcam. But the problem is, you know, you're going to need to find a way to sync these things together and maybe like some sort of video editing software and that's that's really, you know, annoying. The screen recorder that's built into Loom, it's just a Chrome extension and you can record them at the same time. It's always in sync. So, it cuts you down. There's no real learning curve. It just works, right? That's like one supporting argument. Then you could say again even lower, okay, so if you're going to record your update, the next thing you probably do is get to edit it. What if you have a mistake? You got to talk through that. Then you got to bring it into the, you know, iMovie or Final Cut. And so we can basically work our way through and then we're tying specific functionality of our product to show how does it solve the problems of the competitive alternative to build out all the supporting messages. And so Loom actually because they just positioned against meetings, it was a simplified version of this. They were basically saying you're providing updates to your co-workers, you're using meetings, everyone hates meetings. Here's a brand new way. And they didn't have to necessarily tie it one to one. And then they basically walked out the process of using Loom. And so once you get all of these things together, we can start to pull them into a loose wireframe and work section by section to make sure that every one of the boxes gets its due on the homepage. And so when we work with clients, we're basically doing this color coding scheme where we have the boxes from the left, which would be the positioning statement, and then we write the copy, but we always color code it to show, hey, even though the words look different, we've punched up the language, it's more fun or more engaging or whatever it might be, we're actually staying true to the language that we all agreed on, the messages that we agreed on in the previous strategic steps. And so what you don't want to happen is when you start writing copy to be like, "We thought this sentence sounds super cool and accidentally you've completely changed the positioning because someone thought this was a a sexy sounding sentence." So you always try to keep it together like this. And so with someone like Loom, you could potentially get the entire positioning statement re-represented in the first scroll of your page. And so some of this is what Loom actually did. Some of it would be things that we would have changed. But right the beginning it says Loom on, meetings off. So already we're saying don't use a competitive alternative of meetings and then it says record quick videos to update your team and cut down meetings by 29%. So there we're getting our differentiation we're getting the exact workflow or the use case of the product and then at the bottom what we would have added is they said you know trusted by 25 million people. We would have said the screen recorder trusted by and then at that level you've hit all five boxes in a very very clear homepage. And so when you read this, you obviously don't have the same visceral reaction that you would have to the previous ones. You say, "Oh, I get it. I understand what this product's for. Why is it better? Um, what does it do?" All that type of stuff. And then from there, the second aspect of this that we think is important a lot of times is to actually spell out the problem. So we we're a big fan of having a problem section. Some people don't like this because like we don't like being negative, but we think this really helps paint the picture. Having a problem and a solution. So we basically take those red boxes from the left and give them their own space. And so with Loom, it could be something like meetings are super unproductive and waste everyone's time. You spend a ton of time chitchatting. Nobody preps. You need to reexlain the context. In the end, most 30 minutes uh meetings could have been five. And so you're basically setting people up for the other half, which would be this uh we would we would call it the solution intro where you basically repitch the product in the context of the problem that you just explained. And this actually was what Loom had on their page for a while. The easiest screen recorder you'll ever use. Record in a few clicks, share anywhere, collaborate better. And then what we would go to is what we would call the 30-se secondond product demo. In reality, when people read homepages, they don't actually read. They just skim. They're just jumping from headline to headline to headline. Maybe they're reading something for a second underneath. But if you watch people, if you spend so much time crafting the perfect sentences and then you watch a friend use the website and you say, "Hey, check out this company. Don't say it's one that you work for, you know, or they'll just go like this." They'll just get on their trackpad. They're like, "Okay, let me check it out." And they're just scrolling. They're like, "Yeah, this is interesting." And you're like, "You didn't read anything." And they're like, "What do you mean? It's this and this." That's how we engage. So, what we're trying to do is give a we call it the 30 secondond product demo. In reality, it probably has to be a 15-second product demo, which is to hit the main functionality that could be understood in a 15-second scan. So, then we would take the right half of that canvas from before and start pulling them into what we would call product highlights. And so, what we're trying to do is basically say, okay, we set up the problem. We showed you exactly what's the issue with it. Now, let's show how we solve it. And so, these some of these are actual things that Loom did. Again, we're trying to color code based on these messaging elements. things like a feature which would be like one specific part of the product, the capability which is what you do with that feature and then the benefit. It's like why does this matter? What would this help me? What would be the outcome? Like you know the thing that it would change and so lightning fast screen recording record your screen and camera at the same time with Loom's Chrome extension, desktop app or mobile app. Um on the next one, right, editing video is as easy as editing a text. Loom transcribes your videos automatically, letting you highlight and delete what you don't like. And then you know you could do the last two if you had four you could maybe have three. Share your Loom with your whole team. Loom creates a unique link for every video that you can email or text. They can view without creating an account. See who watched and how long. Loom tells you exactly when someone watches your video and how far they got. So you know who needs a gentle follow-up. So we're trying to get the holistic thing in maybe 15 to 30 seconds of a scan, not being too complicated. And then from there, the rest of the page becomes very modular and can kind of highlight specific things to your business. If you have resources that you want to share, that could go there. You need to show people that you have integrations, you could have that go there. But if you can just nail these main ones, the hero main positioning section, the problem and the solution, and the 15-second product demo, you will

topic_hint: The core problem is positioning, not copywriting, explaining different reasons for poor messaging in large vs. small companies.

examples.example_text: Getting in the room with two or 300 like-minded SAS founders for a few days is an incredible experience.

watch this next talk that Pel Gzone gave from the microcom stage into Bravnik last year. In it, he details his sevenstep journey from maker to entrepreneur as he grew balsamic to $6.6 million per year.

key_ideas.idea: Implementing the discussed website messaging elements (hero, problem/solution, 15-second product demo) can lead to top 1% website messaging.
MicroCom events offer an incredible experience for 200-300 like-minded SaaS founders to gather for a few days.
To learn more or get notified about future MicroCom events, visit microcom.com/events.
Pel Gzone's talk details his seven-step journey from maker to entrepreneur as he grew Balsamiq to $6.6 million per year.

transcript: main ones, the hero main positioning section, the problem and the solution, and the 15-second product demo, you will be, in our experience, in the top 1% of all website messaging. So, that is my talk. Thank you all for listening. If you enjoyed that talk, I hope you'll join us at our next MicroCom event. Getting in the room with two or 300 like-minded SAS founders for a few days is an incredible experience. If you want to learn more or get notified when our next event is announced, head to microcom.com/events. If you're on the fence and want to check out another talk to see if it's the right vibe, watch this next talk that Pel Gzone gave from the microcom stage into Bravnik last year.
